data.title: The Browser Extension That Looks Like Google — And Empties Your Crypto Wallet
data.description:
A Fake Google Extension Is Silently Stealing Crypto — Here's How It Works

A malicious browser extension masquerading as a Google note-taking tool has been caught intercepting cryptocurrency transactions and redirecting funds to attacker-controlled wallets — with victims none the wiser until their money is gone. Researchers at McAfee's Advanced Threat Research team published their findings on June 30, 2026, detailing a campaign that combines social engineering, stealthy installation techniques and blockchain-based remote control to pull off one of the more technically layered crypto theft operations seen this year.

What the extension actually does

The extension presents itself as "Google Notes" — a simple, unremarkable tool that blends into the kind of utility software most browser users would install without a second thought. Its actual function is entirely different. Once active, it monitors the user's clipboard in real time, watching for cryptocurrency wallet addresses being copied. The moment one is detected, it swaps it out for an address controlled by the attacker — silently, instantly and before the user pastes it into a payment field.

The attack exploits one of the most reliable blind spots in how people handle crypto transactions. Most users copy a wallet address, paste it and send — rarely stopping to verify that the string of characters they pasted matches the one they copied. Since the vast majority of cryptocurrency transfers are irreversible, a single successful swap can result in permanent, unrecoverable loss.

In cybersecurity terms, this is clipper malware — a category of attack specifically designed to intercept clipboard content and substitute it with attacker-controlled data. Delivering it through a browser extension gives the operators a persistent presence inside the browser environment where most crypto transactions are initiated.

Permissions that don't add up

One of the clearest warning signs in the McAfee report is the permissions the extension requests. For a note-taking tool, the access it asks for is conspicuously broad: all websites, full browsing history and clipboard access. No legitimate notes application requires visibility across every site a user visits or access to their entire browsing history. Those permissions exist to serve the extension's actual purpose — surveillance and interception — not the one it advertises.

An installation method designed to bypass trust checks

The extension does not rely on the standard browser store install process, where submissions are reviewed and extensions must meet minimum trust criteria. Instead, it uses unsigned installers that modify browser preference files directly, allowing it to load as though it were a trusted extension without going through the normal approval process.

Updated versions of Chrome and Edge may still require developer mode to be enabled for unsigned extensions to run, but older Chromium-based browsers — which includes a wide range of Google Chrome derivatives, Brave and Microsoft Edge builds — are more exposed. The operators behind the campaign have also been observed attempting to social-engineer users into enabling developer mode themselves, removing that last barrier to installation.

Blockchain-powered remote control

Perhaps the most technically notable aspect of the campaign is how its operators maintain control over the malware without hardcoding a fixed command-and-control server into the extension itself. Instead, the extension queries a public blockchain smart contract to retrieve its active backend domain dynamically. Domains including devops-offensive.cc and Zebregts.com were recorded during McAfee's analysis.

This approach is deliberately evasive. Traditional malware infrastructure can be disrupted by taking down command servers or blocking known domains. By routing control through a public blockchain — which is immutable and cannot be taken down — the operators ensure their infrastructure remains accessible even if individual domains are blocked or seized. It also makes the extension harder to analyse statically, since no fixed server address is embedded in the code.

The extension also matches intercepted wallet addresses to unique attacker-controlled wallets, rather than routing all stolen funds to a single address. That design makes simple wallet blocklists significantly less effective as a defensive measure.

Who is being targeted

McAfee's telemetry showed infections across multiple regions, with India recording a significantly higher concentration of affected users than elsewhere. The researchers characterised the campaign as opportunistic rather than geographically targeted — the spread reflects where cryptocurrency adoption among consumer users is highest, not a deliberate focus on any one country.

The currencies where wallet address fraud was confirmed include Bitcoin, Ethereum, Bitcoin Cash, Ripple and Dash — covering the most widely transacted assets and suggesting the operators are casting as wide a net as possible.

How to protect yourself

The most reliable defence against this specific attack is manual address verification. Before approving any cryptocurrency transfer, compare the first and last six characters of the recipient wallet address against the original source — ideally on a separate device that is not running the same browser. That comparison breaks the attack: the swapped address will not match.

Beyond that, McAfee advises installing browser extensions only from official stores, auditing installed extensions and removing anything you do not recognise or remember installing, reviewing permissions carefully before granting them, avoiding unsigned software downloads from unofficial sources, and keeping device security software active and up to date.

The broader lesson is one that applies across the category of browser-based threats: extensions that request permissions inconsistent with their stated purpose are a significant red flag, regardless of how legitimate the branding looks. "Google Notes" is not a Google product. The name is designed to borrow trust that was never earned.

Sources

McAfee Advanced Threat Research report, published June 30, 2026. Technical details and telemetry data sourced from McAfee Labs blog. Original reporting shared with Hackread.com. Wallet address verification advice and protective recommendations drawn from McAfee's published guidance.

data.title: The time lag between innovation and productivity explosion: Lessons from the ICT revolution for AI
data.description: The productivity gains brought by AI may be real, but the speed at which they translate into macroeconomic data may not align with the market's most optimistic timeline. Experience from the information and communication technology revolution shows that there is often a significant time lag between the commercialization and investment expansion of new technologies and their explosive productivity growth. Elsie Peng of Goldman Sachs' US economic research team wrote in a study released on July 1st, "We expect AI to significantly boost productivity growth over the next decade." This key point implies that the core variable of AI is not just whether it can improve efficiency, but when that improvement will be confirmed by macroeconomic statistics. The crucial question is shifting from "Is AI useful?" to "When will AI translate into observable productivity growth?" While there is considerable evidence of efficiency improvements at the enterprise and experimental levels, macro-level data typically only begins to reflect these improvements after companies have completed process reengineering, employee training, and organizational adjustments. Information and Communication Technology (ICT) provides an important reference point. Personal computers were commercialized around 1981, and ICT investment began to rise in the early 1980s, but US productivity truly accelerated only in the latter half of the 1990s, a lag of approximately 15 years. AI may be faster, but it won't automatically skip the organizational adjustment phase. The core lesson of ICT: Investment precedes productivity. In the early 1980s, the commercialization of personal computers sparked a wave of ICT innovation. The number of breakthrough ICT patents per capita steadily increased in the late 1980s and early 1990s, and ICT investment also increased significantly starting in the 1980s. Professional services, wholesale trade, transportation, and finance were among the first sectors to increase investment. However, productivity did not rise in tandem. Industry panel analysis shows that for every 1 percentage point increase in the proportion of ICT investment in capital stock, the initial four years actually had a slight negative contribution to projected productivity growth. The positive impact doesn't become clear until around year 8, peaking around year 12 at approximately 0.6 percentage points. This is the "J-curve" in new technology diffusion: companies first invest in equipment, adjust processes, and bear the costs of integration before efficiency can be released. Especially when technology changes information flow, decision-making methods, and employee collaboration, costs often appear first in statistics, not outputs. Three slowing factors: cost, network effects, and intangible capital. The delayed arrival of ICT productivity is primarily related to cost. Semiconductor and communication equipment prices remained high throughout the 1980s, only beginning to decline significantly in the 1990s after increased competition and regulatory changes led to market opening. The 1996 U.S. Telecommunications Act spurred competition in the communication equipment market, increasing competition in the chip sector and further reducing the cost of key components. Secondly, there's the network effect. For technologies like the internet and mobile communications, the value gained by an individual user is limited until the user base reaches a critical mass. In the U.S., productivity gains were more easily realized only after the penetration rate of ICT applications such as the internet and mobile phones crossed the inflection point in the latter half of the 1990s. More importantly, there's intangible capital. Companies need to refactor processes, retrain employees, adjust organizational structures, and build software and data systems. Calculations show that for every $1 invested in ICT hardware, at least $1.70 of intangible investment is required. About two-thirds of this goes to software and databases, with the remainder going to workforce restructuring. This is especially important for AI. GPUs, data centers, and modeling capabilities are easily observed in the market, but organizational restructuring is less noticeable. Historical experience shows that the latter often determines when productivity truly materializes. AI may be faster, but it won't be without friction. A key difference between AI and ICT is their cost curve. The cost of using AI models is decreasing significantly faster than the price of personal computers did in the past. Although some cutting-edge US models have recently increased nominal prices to expand profit margins, competitive pressure and declining underlying computing costs may limit further price increases, and the overall token price path is more likely to stabilize. AI also relies less on network effects than communication technologies. Deploying AI tools within enterprises doesn't necessarily require simultaneous adoption across the entire industry or society to achieve localized efficiency improvements. This allows AI to be reflected in productivity data earlier than ICT. However, slow variables still exist. Enterprises must redo workflows, train employees, and adjust job roles and organizational structures. The proportion of AI-related hardware investment in total capital stock is rising faster than the ICT construction cycle of yesteryear; however, investment in workflow reengineering, measured by the percentage of employee compensation involved in restructuring activities, appears to be slow. Meanwhile, official statistics may underestimate the organizational transformation companies are undergoing. An Atlanta Fed survey of businesses suggests that AI-related intangible capital expenditures will reach approximately $280 billion by 2026. Estimates based on company data indicate that labor costs related to AI transformation in the US could reach $150 billion annually, while organizational capital investment corresponding to executive time allocation is approximately $40 billion annually. Early Signals May Come from Four Types of Industries Looking for early macroeconomic signals of AI productivity cannot be limited to the entire economy. A more viable approach is to first observe industries with high digital infrastructure, high AI exposure, and jobs that are more easily automated or augmented. The report's framework includes four categories of indicators: existing IT exposure, AI adoption rate, AI exposure, and the intensity of job restructuring since 2022. Ranked according to this framework, leading industries include information and data processing, professional services, film and sound-related industries, insurance, credit intermediation, and computer and electronics manufacturing. Among them, information and data processing scored an average of 1.97, professional services 1.48, film and sound-related industries 1.21, insurance 1.09, credit intermediation 0.98, and computer and electronic manufacturing 0.97. If we consider a broader industry perspective, information, professional services, insurance, and finance are the most worthy of priority tracking. These industries have both a higher digital foundation and more tasks that can be automated or enhanced by AI. However, caution is needed in making these judgments. These potentially beneficiary industries have already demonstrated stronger productivity performance over the past few decades. If they continue to outperform in the future, it cannot be simply attributed to AI. More importantly, it depends on whether they are experiencing a new acceleration relative to their historical trends. The market should focus on three main themes. First, cost. As long as the price of model usage and the cost of computing power continue to decline, the resistance to AI diffusion may be less than in the ICT era. Second, organizational capital. Whether enterprises truly refactor their processes, roles, and data systems is more important than simply purchasing AI tools. The ICT revolution has proven that simply buying equipment is insufficient to bring about a productivity explosion. Third, industry productivity inflection points. If information, professional services, insurance, and finance are the first to experience productivity increases exceeding historical trends, they are more likely to provide early evidence of AI entering macro data. However, industry-level productivity data itself suffers from a lag in release. Even if AI has already generated efficiency gains at the micro level, macro-level statistical confirmation may still take several years. The biggest reminder the ICT revolution offers for the AI ​​era is: technological breakthroughs can happen quickly, but productivity gains are usually much slower to materialize.

data.title: Why is the market panicking when Meta starts selling computing power?
data.description:
Author: Seed.eth, BitpushNews

On Wednesday, a Bloomberg report sent shockwaves through the market—a company is building a cloud business and plans to sell its surplus AI to external clients.

What exactly is Meta trying to do?

Actually, Meta's plan to enter the cloud computing market was not a spur-of-the-moment decision.

As early as October 2023, Zuckerberg mentioned this "alternative plan".

As early as October 2023, Zuckerberg mentioned this "alternative plan".

In April of this year, Meta raised its 2026 AI-related capital expenditure forecast to $125 billion to $145 billion. Following the earnings report, its stock price plummeted by over 7%, indicating that the market's patience with Zuckerberg's "splurge first, figure later" approach had run out. At the annual shareholder meeting in May, Zuckerberg explicitly stated that entering the cloud computing market was "absolutely under consideration." He also revealed a key piece of information: "Almost every week, different external companies approach us, hoping we can build API services, or inquiring about whether we have computing power to sell, and are willing to buy it at a premium to our procurement costs." According to information obtained by Bloomberg, Meta is currently considering two main commercialization models: The first is selling "model access permissions"—similar to Amazon AWS's Bedrock service, where developers pay to access AI models deployed on Meta's infrastructure (including its self-developed Muse Spark model), and Meta charges customers based on the number of API calls. The second is selling "raw computing power"—directly renting out raw computing power to external customers, a model similar to that of "new cloud" service providers like CoreWeave. This plan is incorporated into an internal top-level plan called "Meta Compute," jointly led by three key figures: Santosh Janardhan, head of Meta infrastructure; Daniel Gross, head of the AI ​​department; and Dina Powell McCormick, president of Meta. It's worth noting that this move by Meta is not an isolated case. SpaceX, under Musk, also initiated a similar operation this year, selling its idle computing power to Anthropic and Google. Two Interpretations The pessimistic view is that this is the first domino to fall in the oversupply of AI computing power. The reason is simple: if training large models is truly so computationally intensive, why doesn't Meta keep it for its own use instead of selling it? This indicates that Meta's own R&D needs are already insufficient to fill its computing power reserves, meaning the chips it has frantically stockpiled over the past two years may indeed be excessive. What's even more nerve-wracking for the market is that, as one of the industry's "bellwethers," if Meta leads the way in pushing excess computing power onto the market, it means the logic of the "perpetually scarce" AI hardware market will need to be re-examined. If other major manufacturers (such as Google and Microsoft) follow suit, the order growth of upstream suppliers like Samsung, SK Hynix, and TSMC will likely slow down, and they may even face order cancellations. This is precisely why hardware stocks like Micron and SanDisk were heavily hammered—the market was already priced in the expectation that "peak demand has passed." However, the optimistic camp believes this is a complete overreaction. The logic is exactly the opposite: Meta spent hundreds of billions of dollars building a computing power pool, and now it is taking out idle resources that are not currently in use to "replenish its resources." This is just a normal asset management operation and has nothing to do with "shrinking demand." On the contrary, if the cloud leasing model proves successful and generates a stable cash flow, Meta will have more confidence to continue its large-scale purchases of next-generation GPUs, optical modules, switches, and cooling systems—because the money earned from selling computing power can, in turn, support even larger-scale capital expenditures. In short, leasing is for better procurement, not the end goal of procurement. If this logic holds true, then the decline in hardware stocks is an emotional "mistake," and may actually be an opportunity to buy bargains. What do analysts think? According to research reports, most institutional analysts tend to be more optimistic. Bank of America (BofA) reiterated its "buy" rating on Meta in a briefing on Wednesday, offering a very insightful perspective: enterprise AI cloud services essentially insure Meta's multi-billion dollar investment—even if consumer AI business (such as advertising) falls short of expectations, enterprise computing power leasing can provide a safety net, preventing a collapse in profit margins. BofA also estimates that the enterprise AI cloud market is expected to exceed one trillion dollars by 2028, and even if Meta only captures a small slice of this market, it would represent a huge incremental growth for a company that previously relied solely on advertising revenue. New stock market guru Serenity (@aleabitoreddit) believes the market has completely misunderstood the "computing power surplus." Serenity points out that Zuckerberg used an "if" when mentioning renting out computing power, which is itself a hypothetical condition, yet the market has treated it as a given fact and hyped it up. The reality is quite the opposite—computing power is extremely scarce. According to the Financial Times, since March 2026, Google has restricted Meta's access to the Gemini model due to insufficient computing resources, forcing the delay of several internal AI projects at Meta. If even Google is so short of computing power that it has to "cut" long-term clients, how can the entire market possibly be "oversupplied"? This is precisely why Meta urgently signed take-or-pay contracts with Nebius (up to approximately $27 billion over five years) and CoreWeave ($21 billion), totaling $48 billion. Its expenditures are fixed, leaving no room for proactively "cutting" contracts. On the contrary, to reduce its dependence on third parties and spread costs, Meta will most likely continue to raise its capital expenditure guidance, rather than lower it. D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria pointed out the real "victims." He stated, "Meta's move is exactly the same as SpaceX's sale of computing power this year." However, he emphasized that Meta's entry will not directly threaten hyperscale cloud giants like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. Instead, "pure lessors" like CoreWeave and Nebius will be the first to be affected. This is because these companies have largely relied on "wholesale" computing power to large companies like Meta and then resell it. Now that the large companies are doing business themselves, the space for intermediaries is naturally drastically compressed. Who benefits and who suffers? Looking at the interests of all parties, the outlines of the winners and losers are exceptionally clear. The biggest winner: Meta itself. It not only adds another revenue stream, but more importantly, it gives investors peace of mind. After spending $14 billion to poach Alexander Wang from Scale AI last year, Meta's performance in advancing its AI business has been lackluster. With the commercialization of AI slower than expected, this new revenue stream finally gives Meta the opportunity to break free from the valuation constraints of a "pure advertising company" and take a step into the enterprise services market. The biggest losers: CoreWeave, Nebius, and other "computing power sub-landlords". Their survival depends on the fact that "large companies disdain or lack the ability to lease out computing power piecemeal." Now that Meta is directly involved, it means that their most scarce resource—a stable source of computing power—may be cut off. Even more critically, these companies have high leverage and stockpile GPUs, resulting in extremely high financial costs. Once leasing prices are driven down by a "big player" like Meta, their profit margins will be severely compressed. The most uncertain sector: the hardware supply chain (Micron, Corning, SanDisk, etc.). Their fate hinges on a crucial question: will Meta's cloud business reduce its total procurement volume or increase its purchasing power? Currently, Meta's official capital expenditure guidance for 2026 remains at $125 billion to $145 billion, with no signs of downward revision. If the "optimists'" "positive cycle" story comes true, then today's plunge is a golden opportunity; but if the "pessimists'" prediction of "demand peaking" becomes a reality, then the high valuations of hardware stocks may have only just begun to crumble. At the May shareholder meeting, Zuckerberg stated bluntly, "The cloud business option gives us more confidence to continue building AI infrastructure." This statement precisely captures the essence—cloud leasing is the "seatbelt" of the AI ​​strategy. With this seatbelt, Meta dares to continue accelerating in the computing power race. From this perspective, those truly losing sleep are perhaps the middlemen who rely on "computing power scarcity," because the major companies have already begun collecting rent themselves.

data.title: Dogecoin Enters Historically Weakest Period As Bearish Signals Threaten Further Losses
data.description:
Dogecoin is entering what has historically been its weakest trading period of the year, with seasonal trends and deteriorating technical indicators pointing to growing downside risks for the world's largest memecoin.

DOGE was trading around $0.073 after falling more than 3% on the day, extending recent weakness as investors continue to adopt a risk-off approach. Historical data shows June has consistently been Dogecoin's poorest-performing month, with the token recording nine consecutive negative June returns and an average monthly decline of 7.29%.

If the seasonal pattern repeats, DOGE could finish the period near the $0.07 mark, while Long Forecast projects an even steeper decline toward roughly $0.066 in the following month.

From a technical perspective, Dogecoin is hovering just above an important support zone around $0.074 after sustained selling pressure pushed prices lower.

Despite elevated trading volume during the decline, momentum indicators suggest the market has yet to reach deeply oversold conditions, leaving room for additional selling. Meanwhile, resistance remains clustered between $0.080 and $0.085, limiting the scope for any immediate recovery.

Spot $DOGE ETFs have seen two days of inflows in the last month...

According to the latest data from SoSoValue, the three spot @Dogecoin ETFs in the US have seen only two days of inflows since May 19.

Once on June 2 and once on June 17. These two inflow days amounted to just… pic.twitter.com/HYooEJ5M2A

— BSCN (@BSCNews) June 25, 2026

Analysts suggest that maintaining support above $0.074 could allow DOGE to stabilize and potentially rebound if broader market sentiment improves, particularly if Bitcoin regains momentum.

Breakdown could trigger another wave of selling

For now, the most likely near-term scenario appears to be consolidation between roughly $0.074 and $0.082 as buyers and sellers remain locked in a tug-of-war.

Dogecoin Falls 26% And Its Own Account Is Making Yoga Jokes

Dogecoin's (@dogecoin) official X handle quipped about "downward dog" charts even as dogecoin:native shed 26% of its value in a month.

"somebody needs to tell the charts to stop doing the downward dog," Dogecoin… pic.twitter.com/MLLBdkBVEG

— BSCN (@BSCNews) June 26, 2026

However, a decisive move below the $0.074 support level could expose the next major support around $0.070. A break beneath that level may accelerate bearish momentum as traders continue reducing exposure to higher-risk assets.

Dogecoin remains about 82% below its late-2024 peak, highlighting the scale of the correction that has weighed on the memecoin over the past year.

data.title: Robinhood Launches AI-Native Layer-2 Network With 24/7 Tokenised Stock Trading and Global Crypto Expansion Plans
data.description:
Robinhood Is Building Its Own Financial Ecosystem

Robinhood is moving beyond being a trading app and positioning itself as a builder of financial infrastructure, launching its own blockchain network while expanding deeper into tokenised assets, decentralised finance and global crypto markets.

The company officially launched the public mainnet of Robinhood Chain, an Ethereum Layer-2 network built using Arbitrum's technology stack.

Robinhood Chain mainnet is live.

Fast, secure, AI-native, and purpose-built for real-world assets from day one.https://t.co/klNh8iHFPdpic.twitter.com/fvKcmSZPUo

— Robinhood (@RobinhoodApp) July 1, 2026

Alongside the blockchain debut, Robinhood unveiled a series of new products spanning tokenised stocks, decentralised lending, perpetual futures trading and AI-powered crypto services.

Robinhood Chain Launches With DeFi And AI At Its Core

Robinhood Chain has been designed as a permissionless, AI-native blockchain built specifically for real-world assets (RWAs), with the company aiming to create closer links between traditional financial markets and decentralised finance ecosystems.

The network launched with support from several major crypto infrastructure and trading firms, including Uniswap, Pleiades, Alchemy, BitGo and Chainlink.

Uniswap will provide a dedicated automated market maker (AMM) as the chain's primary public liquidity venue, while Pleiades will operate a separate AMM focused on proprietary trading activities.

Robinhood Chain is here, and Uniswap is the primary public AMM from day one

Uniswap Protocol and UniswapX are live alongside support in Uniswap Web App, Wallet, and API

Swap, provide liquidity, and explore the chain built for real-world assets pic.twitter.com/ZYOEh3ES56

— Uniswap (@Uniswap) July 1, 2026

Robinhood said the network will support core decentralised finance functions from launch, including lending and borrowing services, while also enabling AI agents to participate in crypto trading and portfolio management.

Robinhood Senior Vice President and General Manager of Crypto and International Johann Kerbrat said,

"Decentralized finance unlocks possibilities beyond what traditional finance can offer, but historically, it has required technical expertise to navigate.”

He added,

We're bringing the best of traditional finance and DeFi together, and in doing so, expanding financial ownership to every corner of the globe."

How Robinhood's New Stock Tokens Will Operate

A key feature of Robinhood Chain is the introduction of new tokenised stock products, known as Stock Tokens, which allow eligible users to trade shares of major listed companies around the clock through blockchain infrastructure.

The tokens provide economic exposure to underlying equities, including companies such as Nvidia and Apple, while enabling users to deploy these assets into lending pools and use them as collateral across decentralised finance applications.

Stock Tokens are live in your wallet.

Own tokens that track 90+ popular US companies and ETFs like NVIDIA, Google, Apple and more, then put them to work in DeFi.https://t.co/klNh8iHFPdpic.twitter.com/A5hm7AWah7

— Robinhood (@RobinhoodApp) July 1, 2026

Robinhood disclosed that the Stock Tokens are structured as tokenised debt securities issued by Robinhood Assets (Jersey) Limited.

While they mirror the economic performance of underlying stocks, they do not provide holders with legal ownership rights or beneficial interest in the actual shares.

The products are available through Robinhood Wallet to eligible users in more than 120 countries.

Trading access is supported through decentralised exchanges and protocols including Uniswap, Rialto, Lighter, 1inch and Arcus, the latter developed by contributors behind dYdX.

Robinhood Wallet now supports access to perpetual futures on @Lighter_xyz, Apple Pay and Google Pay funding, and Stock Tokens all in one place.

And, for the next 90 days, gas fees on Robinhood Chain are on us, including swaps, bridge transactions, and zero fees on perps. Terms…

— Robinhood (@RobinhoodApp) July 1, 2026

However, the product remains unavailable to users in the United States and faces restrictions in several other jurisdictions, including Canada, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, the United Arab Emirates and sanctioned regions.

From Classic Stock Tokens To A DeFi Ecosystem

The launch builds on Robinhood's earlier tokenised equities offering introduced in June 2025 for users across the European Union and European Economic Area.

That initial product line, now referred to as "Classic Stock Tokens", offered more than 200 tokenised stocks and exchange-traded funds on Arbitrum One.

Those products allowed investors to gain economic exposure to listed companies, receive dividend payments within the app and trade nearly around the clock, while similarly excluding direct ownership rights.

With Robinhood Chain, the company is expanding beyond simple tokenised investing.

Its strategy now centres on creating an ecosystem where tokenised assets can interact with decentralised exchanges, lending markets and collateral systems within a unified blockchain environment.

Robinhood Wallet Expands Into Lending And Perpetual Futures

Robinhood also announced several additions to its crypto product suite.

The company launched Robinhood Earn, allowing eligible users in the United States to lend the dollar-pegged stablecoin USDG and earn annual returns of approximately 7% through decentralised lending mechanisms.

Robinhood Earn is rolling out to eligible US customers.

Lend USDG onchain through a self-custody wallet and earn an estimated 7% APY.

APY subject to change.https://t.co/klNh8iHFPdpic.twitter.com/fQrEZbuVu9

— Robinhood (@RobinhoodApp) July 1, 2026

Meanwhile, Robinhood Wallet users in supported jurisdictions will gain access to perpetual futures trading through integration with decentralised perpetual exchange Lighter.

The company further revealed plans to introduce Agentic Accounts in the United States, enabling AI-powered agents to participate in trading and investment management activities.

Global Expansion Targets Canada, Singapore And The UK

Beyond new products, Robinhood positioned the announcements as part of its largest international expansion strategy to date.

The company has officially entered the Canadian market and secured a capital markets services licence in Singapore, where it plans to launch crypto services in the near future.

We're officially north of the border. 🇨🇦

Robinhood is now live in Canada with zero trading fees for a limited time for fully onboarded clients on the Robinhood app.https://t.co/klNh8iHFPdpic.twitter.com/Xnks2D5thV

— Robinhood (@RobinhoodApp) July 1, 2026

Robinhood also confirmed that cryptocurrency trading products for UK users are expected to launch soon.

The expansion will add to Robinhood's existing customer base of nearly 28 million users worldwide.

Robinhood Shares Rise Despite Recent Crypto Revenue Decline

Investors reacted positively to the announcements, with Robinhood shares climbing more than 8% on Wednesday to close at US$108.65.

The stock has gained nearly 20% over the past month, although it remains approximately 29% below its 52-week high of US$153.86.

The launch comes during a challenging period for Robinhood's crypto business.

The company recently reduced its workforce by around 10% following a sharp decline in cryptocurrency-related revenue, which fell 34% quarter-on-quarter to US$134 million from US$221 million.

Robinhood is scheduled to report its second-quarter financial results on 29 July 2026.

data.title: Meta made a decision; storage prices plummeted.
data.description:
Author: Xiaojing, Tencent Technology

Beijing time, July 1st, foreign media reported that Meta is building a cloud computing business and will sell AI computing power to external customers.

This was not entirely unexpected.

Five weeks ago, at Meta's annual shareholder meeting, when asked if he would compete with Amazon and Microsoft in the cloud computing field, Zuckerberg gave a clear answer: "It's definitely on the table." He also revealed a detail: "Almost every week, external companies approach us, either asking if we can open an API or asking if we can buy Meta's computing power at a higher price." From "under consideration" to "under construction," it only took five weeks. After the news broke, Meta surged, but it "crashed" AI infrastructure stocks in the US stock market. At the close of US stock trading in the early morning of July 2, Meta rose 8.81%, the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index plummeted by more than 6%, Micron Technology fell 10.57%, SanDisk fell more than 11%, Intel fell more than 7%, and ASML, AMD, TSMC, and ARM fell more than 5%. Independent cloud computing providers suffered even more severe sell-offs, with Nebius plunging over 14.5% and CoreWeave falling over 13%. [Image of Nebius and CoreWeave] ... This figure was revised upwards by $10 billion when the Q1 financial report was released at the end of April, from the previous $115-135 billion. Besides building its own data centers, Meta also signed several huge external contracts this year: a five-year, $60 billion strategic agreement with AMD to purchase 6 gigawatts of custom Instinct GPUs; a $21 billion AI computing infrastructure contract with CoreWeave; and a computing power procurement agreement with Nebius for up to $27 billion. These three external contracts alone exceed $100 billion. However, Meta's investment situation differs fundamentally from the other three: Microsoft has Azure, Google has GCP, and Amazon has AWS, whose huge CapEx investments are directly offset by cloud service revenue. Meta does not. Every penny of its previous infrastructure investment was a pure cost item, entirely used to serve its own advertising recommendation system and AI applications; not a single part was a product sold externally. In its May analysis, Sherwood News directly pointed out that compared to other tech giants making similar large-scale investments, it lacks the high-profit cloud business and enterprise revenue to cushion the impact. This also explains an anomaly: Meta exceeded Wall Street's earnings expectations for two consecutive quarters in 2026, yet its stock price is still down about 4% year-to-date. The core question in the market is: where exactly is the return on spending $135 billion a year to build data centers? Zuckerberg's Calculation: Buying Insurance Zuckerberg's original words at the shareholders' meeting were: "We haven't done that yet because we believe this computing power still has a place. But obviously, if one day we feel we've overbuilt, then this is an option; this also strengthens our confidence to continue investing in construction." Two key words: "If we have overbuilt," he himself is leaving himself a way out for the possibility of building too much. "Partially what gives us confidence," the very existence of the cloud option is the basis for his confidence to continue spending money. In other words, Meta didn't build data centers because it wanted to build the cloud, but because it built too many data centers, it needs the cloud as a safety net. In its early June analysis, Datafloq, a technology content platform focusing on big data, AI, and cloud computing, pointed out that this makes it easy for investors to interpret Meta's capital expenditure bet as an either-or judgment—either the internal AI investment succeeds or fails. However, in reality, cloud computing is an option. If the internal AI monetization is successful, all computing power can be used internally, and the cloud business can be discontinued; if internal consumption falls short of expectations, the excess computing power doesn't need to sit on the books for depreciation and can be converted into revenue. It transforms "losing the bet means losing everything" into "losing the bet but still being able to collect rent." But reading the same sentence from the opposite perspective also reveals anxiety. Foreign media commentary was sharp: "If you can't use it all yourself, you'll pass the cost on to others. This isn't something someone confident in the future of AI would say. If Zuckerberg is truly convinced that internal demand can consume all the computing power, he has no reason to allocate valuable GPU resources to external competitors." Meta is missing something for cloud computing; having GPUs doesn't guarantee success. Having a GPU cluster doesn't equate to being able to do cloud business. Meta lacks a list of things: enterprise-grade multi-tenant isolation architecture, security compliance certifications (SOC 2, HIPAA, ISO 27001, etc.), fine-grained billing and SLA assurance systems, global multi-regional deployments and network access nodes, and most importantly, an enterprise sales team and customer success system. Meta has been a purely consumer-facing (to C) company since its inception, never selling anything to enterprise customers and lacking any B2B sales experience. Datafloq's analysis judged Meta's possible path: "Trying to build a full-stack cloud platform is a strategic mistake; the correct approach is narrow-slicing." The article listed four possible product forms: First, raw computing power rental, priced by the hour, with no long-term contracts, and GPU clusters scheduled via API; second, Llama model inference hosting, allowing enterprises to run Llama without building their own GPU infrastructure; third, enterprise model fine-tuning services, fine-tuning open-source models on Meta's hardware using private data; and fourth, agent infrastructure, providing dedicated tool calls, credential management, and audit logs for AI agent workloads. This means that Meta's short-term cloud strategy will most likely involve selling computing power in a "wholesale" manner, targeting a small number of large clients with long-term contracts, similar to the CoreWeave model. It won't be a complete cloud platform like AWS, offering self-registration, on-demand usage, and hundreds of services. The latter requires organizational capabilities and a customer ecosystem that cannot be built up in two or three years. Meanwhile, on May 28th, Meta also did two other things: announced paid subscription tiers for Instagram, WhatsApp, and Facebook, and, according to The Information, established a new "Enterprise Solutions" department, deploying engineers and product managers directly to large enterprise clients to help deploy AI tools. These three events constitute a complete narrative: Meta is systematically seeking revenue streams beyond advertising to support its CapEx bill. Entering the cloud market is just the boldest step in this process. Supply Chain Earthquake: Meta Rises 6%, CoreWeave and Nebius Fall 9% Following this news, Meta rose over 6%, while AI computing power leasing companies CoreWeave and Nebius both fell over 9%. The dramatic drop in CoreWeave and Nebius indicates that the market views this as a repricing of the entire neocloud business model's competitive advantage. The impact is threefold. The first layer is the direct competitive threat. CoreWeave and Nebius' business model is essentially "bulk purchase of GPUs → building clusters → reselling to AI companies at a higher price." High gross margins are predicated on a tight supply of GPU computing power in the market and a lack of alternatives for customers. Meta, the most aggressive investor in computing power, entering the fray adds another player with massive computing power supply. Moreover, Meta's GPU procurement costs are lower than Neocloud's because it has signed strategic contracts worth hundreds of billions of dollars with Nvidia and AMD, securing the best prices. Its selling price can be lower than CoreWeave's, while still remaining profitable. The second layer is more fatal: identity conflict. Meta is currently one of CoreWeave's largest customers. In April 2026, CoreWeave announced an expanded AI infrastructure agreement with Meta, totaling $21 billion, with services extending to 2032. Now that Meta wants to do the same thing, it's like your client announcing they're becoming your competitor. The market's natural reaction is skepticism: will the $21 billion contract be renewed after it expires? Is Meta buying time, waiting until its own cloud business is built so it no longer needs CoreWeave? The third layer is the collapse of the valuation narrative. CoreWeave's IPO story in March 2025 was "explosive growth in AI computing power demand, extreme scarcity of supply, and we are a scarce supplier." This narrative supported its rocket-like growth from zero to a market capitalization of tens of billions of dollars. But Meta's entry into the market to sell computing power directly shakes the core premise of "scarcity of supply." If even the world's largest spender of AI computing power feels it might have surplus computing power to sell, is the overall market supply and demand relationship really as tense as previously portrayed? This doesn't mean CoreWeave's business will collapse immediately. Its Q1 2026 revenue was approximately $2.1 billion, with a large backlog of contracts, ensuring short-term revenue security. However, the capital market prices expectations, not the current situation. When the largest customer is also the largest potential competitor, the long-term growth story needs to be rewritten. Is it a positive or a warning sign? Regarding Meta's move into cloud computing, is it truly a positive development? Those who are bullish believe this represents an upgrade to Meta's investment logic. Previously, Meta's CapEx was a purely one-way bet, betting that AI could significantly increase advertising revenue and user engagement. A win would bring huge returns, while a loss would result in enormous sunk costs. Now, with the cloud business as a pathway, the investment has become "offensive and defensive." The global cloud infrastructure services market reached $128.6 billion in revenue in Q1 2026 (data from Synergy Research Group), with an annualized rate exceeding $455 billion, and AI compute is the fastest-growing sub-sector. Meta only needs to capture a small slice to generate substantial revenue. From a portfolio theory perspective, this transforms Meta's CapEx from a "high-risk single bet" into a "hedging two-way option." Those who are bearish believe this is precisely an "early warning sign" of an AI CapEx bubble. The logic is simple: if Meta truly believes that internal AI demand can absorb all computing power and generate corresponding returns, why would it allocate valuable GPU resources to external competitors? The very act of building a cloud platform is a hedge against the possibility that the internal AI monetization process might not proceed as expected. The four giants' combined CapEx in 2026 is approximately $725 billion, but the incremental revenue directly generated by AI may only be in the tens of billions, resulting in a severe mismatch between input and output. Meta's move into cloud computing may be the most aggressive player preparing for the possibility of computing power oversupply. There is also a technological concern. AI inference efficiency has improved rapidly over the past year, slashing unit inference costs every few months. If the rate of efficiency improvement continues to outpace demand growth, then the data centers built today may not be "insufficient" but "too many." Meta's cloud computing move is insurance against this possibility. On the same day, the US storage sector plummeted. Micron, SanDisk, and others saw declines of around 10%. The core logic behind the surge in these companies over the past year is the "AI data center construction boom driving explosive demand for HBM and enterprise-grade SSDs." Micron's revenue grew by 196% year-on-year last quarter, telling the story of "unlimited demand and insufficient supply." However, this news from Meta directly shakes the underlying assumption of "building first isn't enough." If tech giants' future data center construction pace may slow down, this means a downward revision of expectations for HBM and enterprise-grade storage procurement growth. This is also a story about the AI ​​arms race entering its second half. For the past two years, everyone has been competing to see who dares to spend money, who can secure GPUs, and whose data center is the largest. But even Zuckerberg, who has been the most daring spender, is "afraid." "Having built so much infrastructure, we need to ensure that regardless of whether AI monetization is fast or slow, we won't lose everything." When the biggest buyers start preparing to become sellers, who will still be the real buyers?

data.title: For the first time, the United States truly controlled the offshore financial market through encryption.
data.description:
Two major events that happened yesterday:

1. The EU's Cryptocurrency Act (MiCA) came into effect, and 80% of companies failed to obtain licenses and were forced out of the market. Binance's application was stalled, so it simply withdrew and exited Europe.。

2. On the same day, Binance released a report—its stock trading business had reached $1 billion in assets under management within 30 days of launch.

73% of users are from emerging markets, and you can buy US stocks for as little as $5. The entire tokenized stock industry has a total market capitalization of only 1.2 billion, and this single business line caught up with the entire industry in 30 days. This time, MiCA in Europe didn't issue a license to Binance, and Tether didn't even apply for a European license, essentially handing over the offshore financial market to the US. In the 1950s and 60s, to evade US interest rate controls, the US dollar moved to London, creating a multi-trillion dollar "Euro Dollar" (offshore dollar) market. The US truly had no control over that market: the money was recorded in London banks' accounts, contracts were governed by British law, and Washington had to litigate with the British government to freeze a deposit. Offshore dollars were once a loophole in the dollar system—the US could see it, but couldn't reach it.

What about this time?

Binance's $1 billion in AUM stock is held in custody by the US brokerage firm Alpaca, and liquidation will be handled through the US DTCC.

The USDT paid by users is backed by Tether's more than 100 billion US dollars in US debt, held in Cantor in New York.

Even the laws are American: In 2023, Binance pleaded guilty to a US Department of Justice case, incurring a $4.3 billion fine and also receiving a compliance officer—the US isn't just watching Binance from the outside; it's sending someone to live inside Binance. Unlike Europe, the US doesn't go around issuing licenses and expelling companies because it has found a more efficient way: it doesn't need to regulate the market, it only needs to have the channels. Custody, clearing, and law—with these three reins in hand, you can register anywhere and serve whomever you want.

Moreover, this deal is too profitable for the United States.

With billions of people worldwide, American banks are too lazy to open accounts for them, and American brokerages can't reach their market. Now, Binance is selling US stocks to Jakarta and Lagos for the US, and Tether is putting US dollars into every mobile phone for the US.

Tether sells only US assets and receives only US dollars. It then immediately buys US Treasury bonds with the money. The clients are someone else's, the risks are someone else's, the regulatory costs are zero, and the US Treasury bond purchases and seigniorage go to the US. This fait accompli is being written into law. The first part has been implemented: The GENIUS Act sets the rules for stablecoins, defining what constitutes a legitimate on-chain dollar – Washington decides. Tether didn't resist, adapting itself to the standards – every additional stablecoin issued globally creates an additional dollar of US Treasury bond buying. The second part is underway: The CLARITY Act regulates market structure, how tokenized securities are issued, and how trading platforms operate. In the future, USDT will be a licensed offshore dollar, and Binance will be a licensed offshore brokerage. Once outlaws, they've become overseas branches of the dollar empire. Meanwhile, Europe, once again hampered by its massive bureaucratic system, is now seeing its automotive market devoured by Chinese companies, its AI and related supply chains almost entirely excluded, and in the crypto market, it has the thickest rules but receives the smallest share of the pie.

data.title: Warsh's latest assessment: The Federal Reserve's balance sheet is now almost a "fiscal tool"
data.description:
Author: Xiao Yanyan, Jinshi Data

At the European Central Bank's annual central bank forum in Sintra, Portugal, Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh reiterated his support for reducing the central bank's balance sheet, but emphasized that the pace would not be rushed. He pointed out that this massive balance sheet took approximately 18 years to gradually form, and in his view, its nature is close to that of a **fiscal tool**, therefore the time required to return to a reasonable level cannot be measured in "weeks".

Warsh clearly stated that any action regarding balance sheet reduction must be based on full communication and open preparation. He also emphasized that even while remaining open to a variety of policy tools, **interest rate policy should remain the core tool of the Federal Reserve**. All related decisions will be made jointly by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) and the Board of Governors, rather than by a single level of authority.

Regarding the balance sheet issue, Warsh announced the establishment of a special task force in June and revealed this week that the team will include external members, with the specific composition expected to be announced "possibly next week." In addition to this group, the Federal Reserve has several other working groups studying issues such as data use, productivity and employment, communication mechanisms, and the inflation framework. Warsh's latest statement further reinforces the mainstream view on Wall Street: Substantial balance sheet reduction is unlikely to begin before 2027. Previously, during the global financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic, the Federal Reserve rapidly expanded its balance sheet through multiple rounds of asset purchases, reaching a peak of nearly $9 trillion in June 2022, compared to less than $900 billion in 2007. The CIBC strategist team, including Michael Cloherty, has long believed that subsequent balance sheet reduction will be characterized by a slow and limited pace. The envisioned path is as follows: A relevant plan will be launched near the end of this year, followed by a public consultation phase, which may last until the second quarter of 2027. The actual start of balance sheet reduction will then fall in the fourth quarter of next year. Although Warsh himself has repeatedly advocated reducing the Fed's involvement in asset markets, the market generally holds a cautious attitude towards the feasibility of a large-scale balance sheet reduction. The core concern is that the current financial system is structurally dependent on a high level of reserve supply, and reserves are the counterpart to the Fed's holdings of securities assets and an important source of high-quality liquidity for the banking system. Fitch Ratings has pointed out that a rapid reduction in the balance sheet would pose a risk to the short-term funding market; therefore, this path is both high-risk and unlikely to be a realistic option. Barclays strategist Samuel Earl further warned: “Another outcome is scarcity. The risks of heading towards a state resembling scarcity far outweigh its potential benefits—it could ultimately trigger a repo market meltdown.” In practical terms, the Federal Reserve halted quantitative tightening (QT) midway through last year, instead injecting reserves back into the market by purchasing U.S. Treasury bonds maturing within one year. This adjustment marked a shift in policy focus from passive contraction to proactive liquidity management. In December 2025, the Federal Reserve launched a monthly purchase program of approximately $40 billion in short-term Treasury bonds to alleviate the upward pressure on short-term interest rates. Then-Chairman Jerome Powell stated at the time that this was a “pre-emptive” operation aimed at ensuring adequate reserve levels during the tax season in April of the following year. In April of this year, the scale of bond purchases was significantly reduced to $25 billion, an adjustment exceeding market expectations of a "gradual" reduction. Subsequently, in June, the scale was further reduced to $10 billion, again surprising the market, and remained unchanged throughout the June-July cycle. Based on this pace, since the start of this purchase program on December 12th of last year, the Federal Reserve will have cumulatively purchased approximately $310 billion in short-term Treasury securities by the end of the current operating cycle.

data.title: Bitwise: STRC, MSTR, and the Market Operation Logic at the End of the Cycle
data.description:
Author: Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer of Bitwise; Translated by Shaw, Jinse Finance

The dramatic price fluctuations of STRC are a natural and crucial part of the cryptocurrency cycle. I believe the market is very close to the bottom.

Last week, Bitcoin briefly fell below $60,000, hitting its lowest price since 2024. Many factors triggered this correction, but the most crucial catalyst was the perpetual preferred stock product STRC issued by Strategy (stock code MSTR).

Recently, many clients have consulted me about STRC and MSTR-related issues.

Both clearly reflect the current cyclical stage of the market, so I will address them together here. What is STRC? STRC is a preferred stock product launched by Strategy last year, designed to provide investors with high returns while maintaining or approaching its par value of $100. Initially, STRC offered an annualized dividend yield of 9%. The company promised that if the share price fell below $100, the dividend yield would be increased by 0.25% to 0.50% to attract buying and push the price back to its par value of $100. This mechanism proved effective in the short term: Strategy gradually increased the dividend yield to 11.5%, and the STRC share price remained stable around $100 for a long period. The high returns and low volatility attracted investors, resulting in a cumulative $10.5 billion in investment in STRC. The company then used the raised funds to increase its Bitcoin holdings. What caused this change? In recent weeks, both Bitcoin and MSTR stock prices have weakened, leading investors to worry about Strategy's ability and willingness to continue paying STRC dividends. STRC's stock price plummeted, falling from a face value of $100 to a low of $75. Are investors' concerns justified? There are advantages and disadvantages; it cannot be generalized. From the overall balance sheet perspective, the company's fundamentals are resilient: it holds $49.6 billion worth of Bitcoin, $2.6 billion in cash, total liabilities of $6.8 billion, and $15.5 billion in preferred stock. If it were to liquidate all its Bitcoin holdings now, the proceeds would be sufficient to cover dividend payments for the next 28 years. The core risk lies in the option: **Strategy can voluntarily suspend STRC dividend payments.** While unpaid dividends will accrue interest, there is currently no mandatory payment clause. With the continued decline in Bitcoin prices, **the market is concerned about the company's lack of cash flow to pay dividends, leading to panic selling of STRC.** Did the company ultimately suspend dividend payments? **No.** On Monday, Strategy announced a new capital framework: the company can periodically sell Bitcoin to pay its dividend obligations. It also announced it would no longer support the $100 par value by raising the dividend yield, allowing the STRC price to float freely; furthermore, the company may repurchase STRC shares on the secondary market. This announcement had a significant boosting effect, with both MSTR and STRC stock prices rebounding sharply on Monday. Why didn't Strategy choose to further raise the STRC dividend yield? If the old mechanism were used, it would require raising the yield to an extremely risky, very high level. The original plan only stabilized the $100 par value through small interest rate increases, but when STRC fell to $75, the actual market return had reached 15.4%. To recover its par value, the nominal dividend yield would need to be increased significantly by nearly 4 percentage points from 11.5% to 15.4%. Even so, the effect might not be ideal. A significant interest rate hike could exacerbate market panic, leading investors to question the company's funding sources for interest payments and further depressing the stock price. Once the price falls to $75, it will be unable to recover its $100 par value in the short term. Under the new framework, can STRC's stock price return to $100? Not necessarily. Strategy no longer relies on institutionalized mechanisms to anchor its stock price to $100; although the official dividend yield has been increased to 12%, only a significant surge in Bitcoin prices could potentially bring STRC back to the $100 range. What signal does this send? Market opinions are divided, but I believe Strategy's role in the Bitcoin market has undergone a qualitative change. For many years, it has been the world's largest Bitcoin buyer, continuously providing bullish demand to the market. However, this era of one-way accumulation is likely coming to an end. Going forward, the company will buy and sell Bitcoin flexibly based on market conditions. The key point is that it will not engage in large-scale selling. The existing mechanisms will not force the company to sell billions of dollars worth of Bitcoin annually; once Bitcoin enters a bull market, the company will likely return to a net buying position. However, in the next cycle, its influence on the Bitcoin market will be far less than in the previous one. Who will replace Strategy as the largest Bitcoin buyer? I believe the answer is various institutional funds. Throughout Bitcoin's history, the main buyers in the market have constantly changed: cypherpunks, Asian retail investors, US individual investors, Grayscale GBTC, and MSTR have successively dominated the market. The core driving force for the next round of market activity will be institutional investors—global banks, asset management companies, pension funds, university endowments, sovereign wealth funds, and financial advisors—who control the world's largest pool of funds. Various signs have already confirmed this trend: Morgan Stanley recently launched its own Bitcoin ETF; Wells Fargo has included Bitcoin in its standard asset allocation portfolio; last year, Texas became the first US state to establish a strategic Bitcoin reserve; and several sovereign wealth funds and national banks have already held Bitcoin or launched related research projects. Despite continuous outflows from Bitcoin spot ETFs in 2026, they have attracted over $50 billion in funds since their launch in 2024, and are now offered by most mainstream wealth management platforms. Is there a risk of liquidation or forced liquidation for Strategy? Based on existing data, there is no such risk. The conspiracy theories circulating in the market about liquidation are completely inconsistent with financial calculations. As mentioned earlier, the company has total current assets of $52 billion and total debt of only $7 billion. The price of Bitcoin would need to plummet by more than 70% and remain at a low level for an extended period to jeopardize the company's survival. Bears believe that the $15 billion preferred stock redemption pressure is a long-term burden, but as mentioned earlier, in extreme circumstances, the company has the right to suspend preferred stock dividend payments. What stage does this reflect in the current market? The dramatic fluctuations in STRC coupled with the price correction in MSTR are typical characteristics of the end of a cycle. Whether in the crypto market or traditional finance, each market cycle follows a similar path: bull market rises → investor greed, leverage, and arbitrage using various financial instruments → risk erupts, market reverses; the bottom only appears after the market clears out all excess leverage. STRC is a typical example: funds seeking high returns and low volatility flowed into Strategy, which then used this money to buy Bitcoin. Simply put, funds preferring stable, high returns poured into Bitcoin, an asset with extremely high volatility and no stable returns. This type of capital is inherently incompatible with Bitcoin's risk attributes and must be completely cleared out before the market can find its bottom; we are currently in the process of this clearing out. Similar situations have occurred in the history of the crypto market. During the 2019-2021 bull market, Grayscale's GBTC traded at a significant premium to its Bitcoin holdings for an extended period. Institutions could purchase GBTC at net asset value, lock it up for six months, and then sell it on the secondary market at a 20%-50% premium. This attracted a massive influx of funds into Bitcoin, giving rise to various complex financial arbitrage models. Starting in 2021, the GBTC premium quickly disappeared, various financial arbitrage tools were gradually deleveraged, and the market subsequently bottomed out. I predict that history will repeat itself this time. When will the market bottom arrive? I cannot give a precise time; no one can accurately predict it. Market bottoms can only be confirmed in hindsight. However, several bottoming signals are worth noting recently: First, the MSTR trading price is below its net asset value per share, indicating a complete shift from market greed to panic, a key signal of bottoming out; second, the Crypto Fear & Greed Index has reached historical extremes, entering a period of extreme fear, making it a good time to establish long positions; third, derivatives funding rates remain negative, meaning retail investors' willingness to short Bitcoin far exceeds their willingness to go long. In short, the extreme of market pessimism presents a reversal opportunity. The current market is in a clearing phase, and the problems exposed by STRC are a necessary part of the cyclical adjustment. All market cycles experience this pain, which is unavoidable. The market is still digesting various risks, and I firmly believe that the bottom is near, and a new bull market will begin this fall.

data.title: Prediction market World launched on Solana; Meta Cloud business sells AI computing power.
data.description:
Headline

▌Prediction Market Platform World Launched on Solana

On July 1st, according to official news, the prediction market platform World launched on Solana. This platform allows users to trade contracts linked to cryptocurrency prices, the 2026 World Cup, and other events using the Solana wallet. It will later expand to sports, geopolitical, and macroeconomic markets. The platform is non-custodial; user funds only move when participating in the market. Positions, settlement, and payouts are all completed on-chain, using Phantom's CASH stablecoin as the settlement asset. Chainlink provides the data and settlement oracle infrastructure to reduce reliance on human adjudication.

Meta is reportedly building a cloud business to sell excess AI computing power to external customers. On July 1st, Meta is developing a cloud infrastructure business plan to sell access to AI computing power and models, thus entering into new competition with industry giants such as Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft, and Google Cloud. According to sources familiar with the matter, Meta has been ramping up its acquisition of expensive data centers and other infrastructure to support its artificial intelligence ambitions. Currently, the company is building a new business to generate revenue by selling excess computing power to external customers. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $60,009.47, a 24-hour change of +3.2%; ETH price is $1,608.91, a 24-hour change of +3.4%; BNB price is $550.16, a 24-hour change of +1.5%. SOL price is $77.45, up 6.1% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.07235, up 1.5% in the last 24 hours; XRP price is $1.05, up 2.0% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.3158, up 0.5% in the last 24 hours. WLFI price is $0.05745, a 24-hour change of +0.5%; HYPE price is $62.62, a 24-hour change of -2.2%. Policy: Senator Warren: Legislation Needed to Prevent Trump from Gaining More Crypto-Related Benefits. On July 1st, Democratic Senator Warren stated that legislation is needed to prevent Trump from gaining more benefits related to cryptocurrencies.

▌UN to Lead Global AI Rule-Making

On July 1, according to Axios, a new UN-backed committee will bring together executives from major technology companies and heads of state to jointly develop global rules on artificial intelligence. The UN and its International Telecommunication Union are reportedly forming the "Global Committee on Artificial Intelligence for the Public Good." The committee's first meeting will be held on July 8 in Geneva, Switzerland.

▌Iran: Final Agreement Negotiations Have Not Yet Begun

According to the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA), Iran stated that negotiations on the final agreement have not yet begun. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister discussed strategies to accelerate the implementation of the interim agreement, including measures in Lebanon. Iran, Qatar, and Pakistan held a trilateral meeting in Doha to review the implementation of the interim agreement.

Blockchain Applications

▌Amazon Web Services to Invest $1 Billion in New AI Department

July 1st news: On June 30th local time, Amazon Web Services, the cloud computing service platform of US tech giant Amazon, announced that it will invest $1 billion (approximately 6.8 billion RMB) to establish a new artificial intelligence department, dedicated to assisting customers in building and deploying artificial intelligence systems. Amazon Web Services stated that it expects to deploy thousands of frontline deployment engineers. These teams will collaborate deeply with customers' business, R&D, and security departments, and can help customers build self-sustaining technical teams and deliver complete AI solutions within just a few weeks. Foreign media reports that Amazon Web Services is the world's largest cloud service provider by revenue and the first hyperscale cloud vendor to launch such a dedicated on-site engineer service.

Important Economic Developments

▌Wash says the Fed will pave a new path, reiterates no forward guidance

On July 1st, Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh reiterated that he would not provide "forward guidance" on upcoming interest rate policies, marking a significant shift for the Fed.

“We will be forging a new path,” Warsh said. “I hope that when we meet again in four weeks, we will have a full ‘internal debate.’” At his first press conference last month, Warsh stated that Federal Reserve policymakers unanimously agreed that forward guidance “is not appropriate for the current policy environment.” Regarding whether the Fed will permanently abandon forward guidance, Warsh announced the formation of five special task forces in June, one of which will specifically study communication mechanisms. The other task forces cover topics such as the balance sheet, the Fed’s use of data, productivity and employment, and the central bank’s inflation framework.

▌Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh: News about the appointment of task force leaders may come next week

July 1st news: Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh said on Wednesday that some personnel for its special task force system will soon be in place. Warsh said that starting “next week,” a list of some external experts appointed to the special task forces will be released, and these task forces will be responsible for studying the issues facing the central bank.

... “Some of these people might have sat in these seats in previous years, some might have been academics in the audience, but we really tried to find the best talent…including experts from outside the United States,” he said. On July 1, Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh set an ambitious timeline for the Fed to begin relying on new technologies that provide real-time economic data, thereby reducing its reliance on lagging government survey data. Warsh stated, "My vision is that in the next nine to twelve months, we will leverage new technologies to grasp the dynamics of the real economy synchronously and in real time, enabling us to make more informed decisions; we will no longer rely solely on data from government agencies that are statistically biased and whose survey content is no longer applicable." On July 1st, two weeks after Warsh took office as Federal Reserve Chairman, a senior economic advisor to Trump hinted that the White House might not welcome any interest rate hikes. The Fed's preferred inflation gauge is currently above 4%, and Warsh emphasized that lowering it to 2% is not out of the question. However, in an interview with Fox Business Network on Wednesday morning, White House National Economic Council Director Hassett warned that using interest rate hikes to curb price increases would be a mistake. Hassett believes the economy is in a "supply-driven boom." He pointed out that Warsh holds a similar view, which Warsh emphasized during his campaign for Federal Reserve Chairman last year. “In this context, raising interest rates is simply a macroeconomic mistake,” Hassett said. US President Trump has repeatedly criticized former Federal Reserve Chairman Powell, accusing him of keeping interest rates higher than Trump expected. In recent months, Trump has repeatedly stated that he will give Warsh greater autonomy to formulate policies based on his own judgment. However, Hassett's remarks on Wednesday indicate that the White House still has strong opinions about the Fed's policy-making methods.

▌US Media: US Attempts to Persuade Iran to Abandon Tolls, Claiming Revenue from Sanction Lifting Would Be “Hundreds of Times” Higher

On July 2nd, according to AXIOS, US and Iranian negotiators are meeting in Doha, focusing on the Strait of Hormuz issue. The Trump administration has told Iran that the benefits Iran would gain from the nuclear agreement would far exceed the revenue it could obtain by imposing tolls on the Strait.

The report stated that U.S. Middle East envoy Witkov and Trump senior advisor Kushner met with the Qatari prime minister and other Qatari officials acting as mediators between the U.S. and Iran in Doha on Tuesday. On Wednesday, they met with the Emir of Qatar. Witkov and Kushner have been trying to convey to Iran that its demand for transit fees could undermine a U.S.-Iran agreement that would ultimately be more favorable to Iran. "The message the U.S. is sending to Iran is 'take a long-term view'," said a U.S. official. This official stated that if the U.S. lifts all sanctions under the agreement, the revenue Iran could obtain through the free development and sale of oil and other resources "would be 100 times higher than what they are trying to achieve by charging transit fees."

▌Wash Reportedly Appoints Bessant's Aide as Federal Reserve Advisor

On July 2nd, according to foreign media reports, Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh is expanding his advisory team, with one of the latest appointments being the recruitment of Samantha Schwab, a senior aide to US Treasury Secretary Bessant, as an advisor to the Federal Reserve. Schwab has served as Bessant's deputy chief of staff at the Treasury Department since January 2025 and previously served in the White House during Trump's first presidential term. She also comes from a prominent financial family, being the granddaughter of Charles Schwab, the founder of Charles Schwab. The specific date of her appointment has not yet been determined. This appointment has sparked discussions about a closer relationship between the Federal Reserve and the Treasury Department, especially given Warsh's weekly meetings with Bessant, raising concerns about whether the central bank's independence will be affected.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in July is 71.7%

According to CME's "FedWatch" on July 2nd: the probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in July is 71.7%, and the probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate hike is 28.3%. The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged by September is 36.1%, the probability of a cumulative 25 basis point rate hike is 49.8%, and the probability of a cumulative 50 basis point rate hike is 14.1%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise and Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data.

Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.
